title: How to Read Herb Profiles Without Overclaiming
excerpt: A plain-English guide to reading herb pages on The Hippie Scientist with evidence, safety, and context in mind.

sections.heading: Start with the evidence layer
sections.content: The Hippie Scientist separates practical research notes from marketing language. A strong profile should make clear what is supported by human evidence, what is mechanistic, and what still needs more review.

sections.heading: Use safety notes seriously
sections.content: Herbs and compounds can interact with medications, health conditions, pregnancy, surgery, and other supplements. Use the safety section as a caution signal, not as personal medical advice.

sections.heading: Treat the workbook as the source of truth
sections.content: The site is generated from the project workbook. When a page is incomplete, the fix belongs in the workbook or production queue rather than a hand-edited website patch.

title: Herbs, Compounds, and Context
excerpt: Why plant profiles are more useful when active compounds, mechanisms, safety notes, and evidence tiers are connected.

sections.heading: Plants are not single-ingredient products
sections.content: A plant profile can include many constituents. The compound map helps connect an herb to specific constituents without pretending the whole herb behaves like one isolated molecule.

sections.heading: Mechanisms are not proof by themselves
sections.content: Mechanism notes can explain why something might matter, but they should not be treated as proof of a real-world outcome unless human evidence supports the claim.

sections.heading: Context makes the library more honest
sections.content: A useful profile should show what is known, what is uncertain, and where safety caution is appropriate. That is the difference between an educational library and supplement hype.
